food_subgroup: South Indian legume and vegetable stew

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kumbakonam Kadappa. FOODON_03311737 (stew food product) is the closest defensible parent category covering this moong dal and vegetable stew.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Kumbakonam Kadappa. A0CKP (legume-based dishes) is the closest defensible parent given the moong dal base of this dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Kumbakonam Kadappa or a sufficiently close South Indian legume stew category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Kumbakonam Kadappa is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one medium bowl as typically served alongside idli, dosa, or parotta in South Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Dal and Legume Preparations; Vegetable Curry composite reference
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), Hyderabad, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Kumbakonam Kadappa exists in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are estimated by ingredient-weighted composition using ICMR-NIN published data for cooked moong dal (whole), boiled potato, cooked field beans, carrot, and coconut paste in proportions consistent with traditional Kumbakonam Kadappa recipes. Approximate ingredient proportions used: moong dal cooked 30%, potato boiled 25%, field beans cooked 15%, carrot 10%, coconut paste 8%, onion/tomato/spice/oil 12%. Energy cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Lentil and legume dishes; Atkinson FS et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kumbakonam Kadappa. GI of 45 is estimated by analogy with cooked moong dal dishes (GI 38–52, University of Sydney GI Database) and mixed legume-potato curries (GI ~45–50). The moong dal base dominates the glycemic response; potato contribution is moderated by the legume matrix and coconut fat. GI 45 is a conservative mid-range estimate within the low GI band (≤55). GL per serving = (45 × 13.2 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 17.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Kumbakonam Kadappa contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (630 mg per standard 300 g serving), which represents about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation and monitor portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from potato and moong dal combination
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 45 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 17.8 per 300 g serving, Kumbakonam Kadappa is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; the high dietary fibre (2.6 g/100 g) and legume protein help attenuate postprandial glucose response, but the potato content warrants portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut paste and favourable legume lipid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kumbakonam Kadappa provides approximately 1.9 g saturated fat per 100 g primarily from coconut paste; while coconut-derived saturated fat (lauric acid) has a less atherogenic profile than animal saturated fat, individuals with established cardiovascular disease should limit serving size and prefer preparations with reduced coconut content to keep saturated fat intake within the recommended <7% of total energy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kumbakonam Kadappa is a traditional South Indian vegetarian stew originating from Kumbakonam, a temple town in the Thanjavur district of Tamil Nadu, India. It is a thick, mildly spiced curry made by simmering cooked moong dal (split yellow lentils) with boiled potatoes, field beans or broad beans, and carrots in a freshly ground coconut paste enriched with fennel seeds, coriander, green chillies, ginger, garlic, onion, and tomato. The dish is finished with a tempering of mustard seeds, curry leaves, and dried red chillies in oil. The texture is hearty and semi-thick, with a creamy coconut base that balances the earthiness of the lentils and the starchiness of the potato. The flavour profile is mildly spiced, aromatic, and subtly sweet from the coconut, making it distinctly different from North Indian dal preparations. Kumbakonam Kadappa is classically served as a side dish or dipping curry alongside idli, dosa, or parotta, and is a staple breakfast and tiffin accompaniment in Tamil Nadu. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 98 kcal per 100 g with 4.2 g protein, 3.8 g fat, and 13.2 g carbohydrate, along with 2.6 g dietary fibre per 100 g. The moong dal base contributes plant protein, folate, and iron, while the coconut paste adds medium-chain saturated fatty acids. The estimated glycemic index of 45 places it in the low GI category, making it a relatively diabetes-friendly option when consumed in moderate portions. The sodium content of approximately 210 mg per 100 g requires attention for hypertensive individuals. In Singapore, Kumbakonam Kadappa is served in South Indian tiffin restaurants and is increasingly available through food delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Kumbakonam Kadappa exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by ingredient-weighted composition using ICMR-NIN published data for individual cooked components. GI is estimated by analogy with moong dal dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-documented in culinary literature and the ICMR-NIN component values are authoritative for the individual ingredients used.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are ingredient-weighted estimates derived from ICMR-NIN published tables for cooked moong dal, boiled potato, cooked field beans, carrot, and coconut paste; no single approved-source entry for Kumbakonam Kadappa as a composite dish exists. 2. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN tables for these ingredient categories. 3. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 4. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is 0.0 as this dish contains no animal-derived or fortified ingredients that would contribute vitamin D; confirmed zero by formulation. 5. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; confirmed zero by formulation. 6. Cholesterol (cholesterol_mg) is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; naturally zero. 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked moong dal dishes (GI 38–52); no direct GI measurement for Kumbakonam Kadappa exists in the literature. 8. Sodium estimate of 210 mg/100 g reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home preparations may be lower. 9. The field survey data source price of SGD 21.00 is noted from the input record but is not used as a nutritional evidence source. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are used as closest parent categories; no exact ontology codes exist for this dish.
